app.title: EDPB Breach Notification Assistant
app.subtitle: Offline, privacy-by-design tool for personal data breach triage, ENISA severity scoring and EDPB notification preparation
app.disclaimer: Draft based on the EDPB template v1.0 (public consultation until 5 August 2026). Runs entirely in your browser - no data leaves your device. Not an official submission channel; do not use for live notifications to a supervisory authority until authorities implement the template.
lang.label: Language
pre.title: Pre-assessment (triage)
pre.intro: Quick triage, inspired by the ICO self-assessment, to decide whether this is a notifiable personal data breach before completing the full template.
pre.q_isbreach: Is this a breach of security leading to accidental or unlawful destruction, loss, alteration, unauthorised disclosure of, or access to personal data?
pre.q_personaldata: Does the breach involve personal data (information relating to an identified or identifiable person)?
pre.q_risk: Is the breach likely to result in a risk to the rights and freedoms of individuals?
pre.q_highrisk: Is the breach likely to result in a HIGH risk to individuals?
pre.verdict.notbreach: This does not appear to be a personal data breach under the GDPR. Keep an internal record anyway.
pre.verdict.norisk: Personal data breach with no likely risk: notification to the supervisory authority may not be required, but you must record it (Art. 33(5)).
pre.verdict.notify_dpa: Likely notifiable to the supervisory authority within 72 hours (Art. 33).
pre.verdict.notify_subjects: High risk: also communicate to the affected individuals without undue delay (Art. 34), unless an Art. 34(3) exemption applies.
enisa.title: ENISA severity assessment
enisa.intro: Computes SE = DPC x EI + CB and the severity band. A reproducible score to answer the template field 'describe the methodology used'.
enisa.trigger_off: SE < 2 (Low): notification to the authority is generally not required, but record the breach.
enisa.prefill: Use this score to pre-fill the EDPB notification
form.title: EDPB notification
form.intro: The full EDPB template, prepopulated from your pre-assessment and ENISA score. Conditional fields appear based on your answers.
form.export_note: The exported JSON validates against the open breach-notification schema and can be submitted via the open API by your management system.
record.title: Breach record (accountability)
record.intro: A register of ALL breaches, including non-notifiable ones, inspired by the Canadian PIPEDA record-keeping obligation and GDPR Art. 33(5). Stored locally in your browser.
f.3.label: Type of notification
f.3.tip: Indicate here if you notifying a new data breach or if you are amending a previous notification
f.4.label: Sub-type of notification
f.4.tip: Indicate here if you provide a complete notification, amend a preliminary (incomplete) notification or provide preliminary (incomplete) information to be amended.Complete (all required information can be provided)Incomplete (not all information can be provided right now)Withdraw (Cancel a previous Notification; e.g. duplicate, no risk after initial assessment)
f.5.label: Reasons for withdrawing a previous notification
f.6.label: ID of previously notified personal data breach
f.6.tip: Please provide the ID of the previous personal data breach your are completing or amending.
f.7.label: Data controller's internal reference number
f.7.tip: Provide your internal reference number if any
f.10.label: Type of Identifier
f.11.label: Identifier
f.12.label: Name of the organisation
f.13.label: Contact details*
f.14.label: Sector
f.15.label: Type of organisation
f.15.tip: If you consider your organisation not fitting in those four categories, please select “Others” and specify how.
f.16.label: Further description of organisation type
f.17.label: Classification of economic activity
f.18.label: Name of representative in EEA
f.19.label: Contact details* of representative in EEA
f.21.label: Name of the reporting person
f.22.label: Contact details
f.23.label: Function of the reporting person
f.24.label: Description of the function of the reporting person
f.25.label: National identification number
f.26.label: Accuracy & Responsibility
f.26.tip: The reporting person submits the notification on behalf of the controller; responsibility for the accuracy and completeness of the information provided remains with the controller (Articles 24 and 33(1) GDPR).
f.29.label: Is a data protection officer designated?
f.30.label: Name of the DPO
f.31.label: Contact details*
f.33.label: More information about the incident can be obtained at
f.34.label: Name of the contact person
f.35.label: Function of the contact person
f.36.label: Contact details*
f.38.label: Are other parties such as data processors or joint controllers involved?
f.38.tip: Indicate here if other organisations were involved in the data breach (for example a data processor, a joint controller, etc.)
f.39.label: Qualification of the other involved party
f.40.label: Name of the other involved party
f.41.label: Contact details*
f.44.label: When did the personal data breach occur?
f.44.tip: If you do not know the dates precisely, please indicate the estimated dates below
f.44.opt.b: From a date to a date
f.44.opt.c: From a date and is still ongoing
f.45.label: Only estimated dates are possible
f.45.tip: Please indicated how you estimated the dates of the breach in field 49
f.46.label: Beginning date and time of personal data breach
f.47.label: Ending date and time of personal data breach
f.48.label: Date and time of awareness of the data controller of the personal data breach
f.49.label: Reasons for late notification of the personal data breach
f.50.label: How was the personal data breach discovered?
f.50.opt.b: Detection and communication by the data processor
f.51.label: Further description of the discovery of the personal data breach
f.51.tip: Please describe how the personal data breach was discovered, e.g., Malware or Intrusions detecion system, Coordinated Vulnerability Disclosure, …
f.52.label: Date of notification by other party
f.52.tip: If you do not know this date precisely, please indicate the approximate date.
f.53.label: Further comments on timeline and additional information
f.53.tip:
Please provide an overview over relevant events and other issues regarding the timeline (if applicable), such as, the duration of impacts on availability, integrity and confidentiality, the awareness date of the processor, etc.
Please indicated how you estimated the dates of the breach (if applicable)
f.55.label: Nature of the personal data breach
f.55.opt.a: Confidentiality breach - where there is an unauthorised or accidental disclosure of, or access to, personal data.
f.55.opt.b: Integrity breach - where there is an unauthorised or accidental alteration of personal data.
f.55.opt.c: Availability breach - where there is an accidental or unauthorised loss of access to, or destruction of, personal data.
f.56.label: Type of confidentiality breach
f.56.opt.a: Personal data are exfiltrated or disclosed
f.56.opt.b: Personal data are likely exfiltrated or disclosed (no evidence)
f.56.opt.c: Personal data are NOT exfiltrated NOR disclosed (reasonable evidence present)
f.57.label: Was the data unintelligible to anyone who was not authorised to access it  or were individuals not identifiable?
f.57.tip: If you answered d), please specify why the assessment is not possible (e.g. technical reasons)
f.57.opt.a: Yes and protection is still intact (e.g. data was securely encrypted or otherwise protected)
f.57.opt.b: Protective measures had been taken, but it is likely that they could be  subverted  (e.g. because the unauthorised party could be able to use decryption keys or exploit a technical weakness in the measures that have been applied)
f.58.label: Type of integrity breach
f.58.opt.a: Alteration or modification of the data, with no evidence of illegal or wrong use
f.58.opt.b: Alteration or modification of the data, with evidence of illegal or wrong use, but with the possibility of reversing/recovering the damages to data subjects
f.58.opt.c: Alteration or modification of the data with evidence of illegal or wrong use, without the possibility of reversing/recovering damages to the data subjects
f.59.label: Type of availability breach
f.59.tip: Please assess the availability issue form the data subject’s perspective
f.60.label: Nature of the incident
f.60.opt.a: Abuse of access privileges by employee
f.60.opt.e: Security vulnerability (likely) exploited (known CVE or zero day)
f.60.opt.f: Unauthorised access to personal data in IT systems
f.60.opt.g: Data exfiltration / unauthorised extraction of personal data
f.60.opt.h: Data of wrong data subject shown (mix-up of records)
f.60.opt.j: E-waste (personal data still present on obsolete device)
f.60.opt.k: Incorrect disposal of personal data on paper
f.60.opt.n: Incorrect access permissions (e.g., cloud storage, shared folder)
f.60.opt.o: Paper lost or stolen or left in insecure location
f.60.opt.q: Personal data displayed to wrong recipient
f.60.opt.s: Sending email to multiple recipients without blind copy / with open distribution list
f.60.opt.v: Unintended publication (e.g., public link / public website / shared drive)
f.60.opt.w: Verbal unauthorised disclosure of personal data
f.61.label: Cause of the personal data breach
f.62.label: Further description of the nature of the incident
f.62.tip: Please describe the nature of the breach (detection, awareness, cause, measures in place) shortly and provide on what facts your decision is grounded. If possible indicate the root cause of the incident.
f.63.label: Description of the systems, software, services, and infrastructures involved in the personal data breach and where they are located
f.63.tip:
Please list the descriptions them in semantic groups, e.g. 

Systems:
• production database :
  o client database (full/partial) that countains ****, located in ***
  o internal HR database (full/partial) that countains ****, located in ***
• test infrastucture
  o testing database (full/partial) that countains ****; located in ***
Software:
• Accounting software
• Payment system software
f.65.label: Categories of concerned data subjects
f.65.tip: If you answered i), please indicate what kind of vulnerable people. If you are not sure if or how the concerned data subject are vulnerable, please describe them shortly and why you would consider them as vulnerable.
f.65.opt.i: Vulnerable individuals (elderly, refugees, disabled people, victims, …)
f.65.opt.j: Additional concerned data subjects (please specify in further description)
f.66.label: Further description of categories of concerned data subjects and additional data subjects
f.67.label: Data subjects concerned by the personal data breach
f.68.label: Number of data subjects concerned by the personal data breach
f.70.label: Type of breached data
f.70.opt.a: Basic data (e.g. name, surname, date of birth)
f.70.opt.b: Contact details (e.g. address, phone number, e-mail)
f.70.opt.d: Criminal convictions, offence or security measures
f.70.opt.f: Data revealing racial or ethnic origin
f.70.opt.g: Data revealing religious or philosophical beliefs
f.70.opt.h: Data revealing sex life or sexual orientation
f.70.opt.k: Health data (e.g. medical records, test results, …)
f.70.opt.m: Employment related health data (e.g. proof of sick leave, proof of medical attendance, results of medical fitness tests or similar)
f.70.opt.n: Identification data (e.g. National identification number, ID number)
f.70.opt.q: Payment methods (e.g. credit card, bank accounts)
f.70.opt.r: Profile data (e.g. social network, credit score, psychology or other profile
f.70.opt.s: s) User credentials (e.g. usernames, passwords, tokens)
f.70.opt.u: Additional data types (please specify in further description)
f.71.label: Further description of breached data and additional data categories
f.72.label: Personal data records concerned by the personal data breach
f.72.tip: Please specify the type of record: number of data about each concerned data subject. For instance, to avoid just mentionning “500”. Because “5 types of data (namely *,*,*,*,*,*) about 100 people” and “100 types of data (namely *,*,*,*,*) about 5 people” may not lead to the same risks assessment, even if it could both be sumed up to “500 records”.
f.73.label: Number of personal data records concerned by the personal data breach
f.73.tip: Please indicate here the number of records concerned by the breach, meaning the number of single entries in the database concerned by the breach.
f.75.label: Relevant measures in place when the personal data breach occurred
f.75.tip:
This list is not exhaustive and just covers common measures. 

You may have not applied some measures because they were not applicables or useful.

At the same time, you may have applied non-listed measures that were useful. If so, please specify them with answer L.
f.75.opt.d: Data protection and information security policies
f.75.opt.l: Other measures (please specify in further description)
f.76.label: Further description of relevant measures in place when the personal data breach occurred
f.76.tip: Reminder: You indicated that technical measures are likely to be subverted by the adversaries. Please add the relevant level of detail here to these measures, along the description of the other relevant measures.
f.79.label: Likely consequences in case of breach of confidentiality
f.79.opt.a: Data were disclosed beyond the scope of the privacy policy or relevant legislation
f.79.opt.b: Data may be linked, without unreasonable effort, with other information regarding data subjects
f.79.opt.c: Data may be used for purposes other than the intended purpose or unlawfully
f.80.label: Description of other confidentiality consequences
f.81.label: Likely consequences in case of breach of integrity
f.81.opt.a: Data may have been modified and used even though it is no longer valid
f.81.opt.b: Data may have been modified into otherwise valid data and subsequently used for other purposes
f.82.label: Description of other integrity consequences
f.83.label: Likely consequences in case of breach of availability
f.83.opt.b: Service disruptions and difficulties in service use
f.84.label: Description of other availability consequences
f.85.label: Nature of the potential impact for the data subject
f.85.tip: Please indicate here the nature of the impact on the data subject rights and freedoms.
f.85.opt.a: Loss of control of their personal data
f.85.opt.i: Loss of confidentiality of personal data protected by professional secrecy
f.85.opt.j: Disclosure of data to unauthorised third parties
f.85.opt.m: Material or non-material damage to property
f.85.opt.n: Other impact (please specify in further description)
f.86.label: Further description of the other impacts for the data subjects
f.87.label: Severity of the potential impacts
f.87.tip: Indicate here the result of your self-assessment of the severity of the impact of the breach for the data subjects. Please select the highest severity if multiple potential impacts can occur.
f.89.label: Outcome of the risk assessment carried out by the controller
f.89.opt.a: The personal data breach is likely to result in a high risk to the rights and freedoms of natural persons
f.89.opt.b: The personal data breach is likely to result in a (not high) risk to the rights and freedoms of natural persons
f.89.opt.c: The personal data breach is unlikely to result in a risk to the rights and freedoms of natural personsc) Additional information is needed to assess the risk to the rights and freedoms of natural persons
f.90.label: Further description  of the risk assessment carried out by the data controller
f.90.tip: Please describe the metodology used and the relevant factors you put into account.
f.92.label: Measures taken to address the personal data breach and to mitigate its consequences
f.92.tip: Please indicate to what extend the measures resolve the issue.
f.94.label: Relevant measures taken to prevent similar personal data breach
f.94.opt.e: Data protection and information security policies
f.94.opt.o: Other measures (please specify in further description)
f.95.label: Further description of relevant updated permanent measures to avoid similar incidents in the future
f.95.tip:
Please indicate to what extend the measures resolve the issue and improve the situation.

A description is expected if you answered with “Other measures” in the previous question m).

Nevertheless, do not hesitate to describe further measures taken after the breach, or to be taken, if you think it is relevant.
f.97.label: Has the personal data breach been communicated to data subjects?
f.97.tip:
If you have not assessed yet if you are going to notify, please note that you will have to make a follow-up notification once you have make your decision

If you communicate the data breach to the data subjects in phases or if you cannot reach out to all data subjects, yet. Please indicated that in field 98 and 98a.
f.97.opt.b: No, but it will be communicated on a specific date b1) No, but it will be communicated on a date to be determined
f.97.opt.c: No, the investigation is still ongoing
f.97.opt.d: No, it will not be communicated since the personal data breach is unlikely to result in a high risk to the rights and freedoms of natural persons
f.97.opt.e: No, it will not be communicated since one of the conditions referred to in article 34(3) of the GDPR is met
f.98.label: Date of when information was given to data subjects
f.98.tip: Please indicate here the date on which you have started to inform the data subjects.
f.99.label: Future date on which the data breach will be communicated to the data subjects
f.100.label: Further information about conditions referred to in article 34(3) of the GDPR that are met
f.100.opt.a: The controller has implemented appropriate technical and organisational protection measures, and those measures were applied to the personal data affected by the personal data breach, in particular those that render the personal data unintelligible to any person who is not authorised to access it, such as encryption.
f.100.opt.b: The controller has taken subsequent measures which ensure that the high risk to the rights and freedoms of data subjects referred to in paragraph 1 is no longer likely to materialise.
f.100.opt.c: It would involve disproportionate effort. In such a case, there shall instead be a public communication or similar measure whereby the data subjects are informed in an equally effective manner.
f.101.label: Further description why the conditions referred to in article 34(3) of the GDPR that are met and the measures taken
f.102.label: Number of data subjects informed
f.102.opt.a: All data subjects have been informed
f.102.opt.b: Not all data subjects have been informed
f.103.label: Further description why not all data subjects have been informed
f.103.tip:
Please specify the reason why some data subjects have not been informed, e.g. some might not have been because reason A, some other because reason B, etc.

Reasons can be of various natures and not just the technical ones have to be considered: ongoing process, some data subjects cannot be reached the same way, additionnal laws apply to them, the cost of information is much higher, etc
f.104.label: Means of communication used to inform the data subject
f.105.label: Further description of the other means of communication
f.106.label: Content of the information provided to the data subjects
f.106.tip: Please provide this content in the most relevant way. For example, if the provided information is just an email with only plaintext, copying the text here is sufficent. If, for instance, your information contains pictures/videos useful for understanding, please provide the means for the data protection authority to consult it (website page, email screenshots, download page, etc).
f.109.label: Has the incident been reported to the police/judicial authorities as a criminal offence?
f.110.label: Has the incident been notified to other supervisory authorities or control bodies under other relevant legislation? (e.g. national cyber security centre, etc.)
f.111.label: Further information about notification to other authorities or bodies
f.111.tip: Please specify which authority (about data protection or not) and if possible, provide the case ID that this authority used and then can be referred to.
f.113.label: Does the personal data breach involve cross-border processing carried out by a controller established in the European Economic Area (EEA)?
f.114.label: The lead supervisory authority is:
f.115.label: List of EEA countries where the controller has an establishment
f.115.tip: Indicate here the countries concerned by the breach.
f.116.label: List of EEA countries where affected data subjects are located
f.116.tip: Indicate here the countries concerned by the breach.
f.117.label: Approximate number of data subjects per country
f.118.label: List of EEA supervisory authority to which the breach has been or will be notified
f.118.tip: Indicate here the list of other EU Supervisory Authorities you have or you plan to notify.
f.118.opt.a: - Der Landesbeauftragte für Datenschutz und Informationsfreiheit Mecklenburg-Vorpommern Germany (North Rhine-Westphalia) - Landesbeauftragte für Datenschutz und Informationsfreiheit Nordrhein-Westfalen Germany (Rhineland-Palatinate) - Der Landesbeauftragte für den Datenschutz und die Informationsfreiheit Rheinland-Pfalz Germany (Saarland) - Unabhängiges Datenschutzzentrum Saarland / Landesbeauftragte für Datenschutz und Informationsfreiheit Germany (Saxony) - Sächsische Datenschutz- und Transparenzbeauftragte Germany (Saxony -Anhalt) - Landesbeauftragte für den Datenschutz Sachsen-Anhalt Germany (Schleswig-Holstein) - Landesbeauftragte für Datenschutz Schleswig-Holstein / Unabhängiges Landeszentrum für Datenschutz Schleswig-Holstein Germany (Thuringia) - Thüringer Landesbeauftragte für den Datenschutz und die Informationsfreiheit Greece - Αρχή Προστασίας Δεδομένων Προσωπικού Χαρακτήρα Hungary - Nemzeti Adatvédelmi és Információszabadság Hatóság Iceland - Persónuvernd Ireland - Data Protection Commission / An Comisiún um Chosaint Sonraí Italy - Garante per la protezione dei dati personaliLatvia - Datu valsts inspekcijaLiechtenstein - Datenschutzstelle Lithuania - Valstybinė duomenų apsaugos inspekcija Lithuania - Žurnalistų etikos inspektoriaus tarnyba Luxembourg - Commission Nationale pour la Protection des Données / Nationale Kommission für Datenschutz Großherzogtum Malta - Office of the Information and Data Protection Commissioner Norway – Datatilsynet The Netherlands - Autoriteit Persoonsgegevens Poland - Urząd Ochrony Danych Osobowych Portugal - Comissão Nacional de Proteção de Dados Romania - Autoritatea Naţională de Supraveghere a Prelucrării Datelor cu Caracter Personal Slovakia - Úrad na ochranu osobných údajov Slovenskej republiky Slovenia - Informacijski pooblaščenec Spain - Agencia Española de Protección de Datos Sweden - Integritetsskyddsmyndigheten
f.120.label: Does the personal data breach involve processing, to which the GDPR applies, carried out by a controller that is not established in the European Economic Area (EEA)?
f.121.label: List of EEA countries where affected data subjects are located
f.121.tip: Indicate here the countries concerned by the breach.
f.122.label: Approximate number of data subjects per country

f.123.tip: Indicate here the list of other EU Supervisory Authorities you have or you plan to notify.

f.125.label: Please identify the attachments to this notification
f.125.tip:
Tooltip: with regard to phishing there needs to be a split-up of communications, namely, 3 categories: 
The owner, datasubject of the mailbox
Data subjects that received a phishing-mail

Data subjects of which personal data was in the compromised mailbox
f.125.opt.a: Dated copy of the communication to the datasubject
f.125.opt.c: Dated copy of the research report into the data breach (cyberincidents)
f.125.opt.f: Dated copy of the internal procedure to notify a data breach
f.125.opt.g: Dated copy of the internal policy to delete/destroy (outdated) personal data
f.125.opt.h: Dated copy of the communication to the wrong recipients
f.125.opt.i: Dated copy of the external notification/message of the data breach
f.126.label: Other attachements
enisa.dpc.label: Data Processing Context
enisa.ei.label: Ease of Identification
enisa.ei.lvl.maximum: Maximum - data directly identifies the subject
enisa.cb.confidentiality.label: Loss of confidentiality
enisa.cb.integrity.label: Loss of integrity
enisa.cb.availability.label: Loss of availability
enisa.band.low.meaning: Individuals either will not be affected or may encounter a few inconveniences.
enisa.band.medium.meaning: Individuals may encounter significant inconveniences, which they will be able to overcome despite some difficulties.
enisa.band.high.meaning: Individuals may encounter significant consequences, which they should be able to overcome albeit with serious difficulties.
enisa.band.very_high.meaning: Individuals may encounter significant, or even irreversible, consequences which they may not overcome.
enisa.mit.single_subject: Single data subject / very low volume
enisa.mit.recipient_confirms_deletion: Wrong recipient confirms deletion of the data
enisa.mit.strong_encryption: Strong encryption / data unintelligible to recipient
enisa.mit.professional_secrecy: Recipient bound by professional secrecy / legal confidentiality
enisa.mit.availability_restored: Availability quickly restored from backup (short outage)
enisa.mit.note: Mitigating circumstances that lower the score. Per the accountability principle (Art. 5), these are EDITABLE suggestions, not an imposed black-box adjustment: each toggle moves the indicated variable in the indicated direction; the controller may override. Exact numeric steps are ENISA-default and pending confirmation against the ENISA 2013 PDF.
enisa.provenance: SE < 2 = Low is verbatim from the ENISA document text. The upper cutoffs (Medium 2-<3, High 3-<4, Very High >=4) are the standard, universally-cited ENISA bands but were not extracted verbatim from this PDF's text layer; treat as 'ENISA standard consolidated bands'.
rk.circumstances_cause: Circumstances and cause of the breach (if known)
rk.date_or_period: Date or period of the breach (or approximation)
rk.personal_information: Description of the personal information involved (to the extent known)
rk.mitigation_steps: Steps taken to reduce/mitigate the risk of harm
rk.notification_steps: Steps taken or planned to notify affected individuals (where applicable)
rk.retention_note: Minimum 24 months after the day the organisation determines the breach occurred (SOR/2018-64).
help.dpc: Data Processing Context: how critical the data is. 1 simple, 2 behavioural, 3 financial, 4 sensitive (special categories).
help.ei: Ease of Identification: how easily the data identifies a person (multiplies DPC). 0.25 negligible to 1 maximum.
help.cb: Circumstances of the Breach: added points for loss of confidentiality, integrity, availability (each 0 / +0.25 / +0.5) and malicious intent (+0.5).
record.aggregate_note: Aggregate export: anonymised counts by taxonomy, k-anonymity
obl.title: Your obligations for this breach
obl.intro: Indicative guidance generated from the data you entered.
obl.auth.title: Notify the supervisory authority
obl.auth.required: Required unless the breach is unlikely to result in a risk to the rights and freedoms of natural persons (Art. 33(1)). Deadline: without undue delay and, where feasible, within 72 hours of becoming aware.
obl.auth.notreq: Not required only if the breach is unlikely to result in a risk to the rights and freedoms (Art. 33(1)). Document the reasoning (Art. 33(5)).
obl.auth.assess: Assess whether the breach is likely to result in a risk. The ENISA severity score informs this assessment but does not, on its own, determine the legal obligation.
obl.subj.title: Communicate to the affected individuals
obl.subj.text: Required only if the breach is likely to result in a HIGH risk to the rights and freedoms (Art. 34(1)), without undue delay. Exemptions (Art. 34(3)): (a) data unintelligible, e.g. strong encryption; (b) subsequent measures making the high risk no longer likely; (c) disproportionate effort -> public communication instead.
obl.doc.title: Document the breach internally
obl.doc.text: Always required, for ALL breaches, including those not notified (Art. 33(5)). GDPR sets no retention period; a minimum of 24 months is recommended on the Canadian model (PIPEDA, SOR/2018-64), as proposed in the position paper (Part III.5).
obl.disclaimer: This is indicative guidance generated from the data you entered; it is not legal advice and does not replace the controller's case-by-case assessment under Articles 33 and 34 GDPR. The controller remains responsible for the determination and for the values declared (accountability, Art. 5(2)).
enisa.select_prompt: Select DPC and EI to compute the severity.
record.aggregate_note2: (small cells suppressed). No single-notification rows, no controller identity.
form.methodology_prefix: ENISA severity methodology: SE = DPC x EI + CB =
